message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Coronaviruses (CoV) are a large family of viruses that are common in people and many animal species. Animal coronaviruses rarely infect humans with the exceptions of the Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS-CoV), the Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus (SARS-CoV), and now SARS-CoV-2, which is the cause of the ongoing pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). Many studies suggested that genetic variants in
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    gene may influence the host susceptibility/resistance to SARS-CoV-2 virus according to the functional role of ACE2 in human pathophysiology. However, all these studies have been conducted
                    <jats:italic>in silico</jats:italic>
                    based on epidemiological and population data. We therefore investigated the occurrence of
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    variants in a cohort of 99 Italian unrelated individuals clinically diagnosed with coronavirus disease 19 (COVID-19) to experimental demonstrate allelic association with disease severity.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>By whole-exome sequencing we analysed 99 DNA samples of severely and extremely severely COVID-19 patients hospitalized at the University Hospital of Rome “Tor Vergata” and Bambino Gesù Hospital in Rome.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We identified three different germline variants, one intronic (c.439+4G>A) and two missense (c.2158A>G, p.Asn720Asp; c.1888G>C, p.Asp630His), in 26 patients with a similar frequency between male and female and a not statistically different frequency, except for c.1888G>C, (p.Asp630His) with the ethnically matched populations (EUR).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our results suggest that there is not any
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    exonic allelic association with disease severity. It is possible that rare susceptibility alleles are located in the non-coding region of the gene able to control
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    gene activity. It is therefore of interest, to explore the existence of
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    susceptibility alleles to SARS-Co-V2 in these regulatory regions. In addition, we found no significant evidence that
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    alleles is associated with disease severity/sex bias in the Italian population.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: No evidence for allelic association between Covid-19 and ACE2 genetic variants by direct exome sequencing in 99 SARS-CoV-2 positive patients

reference.article-title: Functional assessment of cell entry and receptor usage for SARS-CoV-2 and other lineage B betacoronaviruses
